mechanism: Participants study lists of words that are semantic associates of a critical word not presented; during recall/recognition, the unpresented critical word is falsely retrieved or recognized. The paradigm relies on automatic spreading activation through associative networks whereby retrieval of list items primes the critical nonpresented word, producing confusion between intended versus generated responses.

Temporal sequences of items are encoded with dual representations: order information via positional/ordinal codes and timing information via a timing signal (neural oscillators). Retrieval uses timing cues to reconstruct position; grouping manipulations create hierarchical temporal representations. The system can weight temporal and ordinal dimensions differentially during retrieval based on task demands.
distribution: output times conditional on input times

mechanism: Repeated items in rapid serial visual presentation compete for conscious awareness based on total neural activation; repetition sharpens item representation (higher signal, lower noise, reduced total activation), reducing competitiveness. Participants use partial orthographic information to discriminate and guess repetitions when task structure permits; guessing masks true repetition blindness.
domain: Visual perception, repetition blindness, word recognition

mechanism: Decision maker updates subjective beliefs via Bayes' rule after each round, adapting estimates of task parameters (success probability or number of choice trials). Expected value maximization identifies target trial; probabilistic response rule determines play/stop. Model accounts for both stationary (sampling-with-replacement) and nonstationary (sampling-without-replacement) mental representations of the stochastic environment.

Causal judgment reflects the goal of accounting for observed occurrences of outcomes. Two types of evidence influence judgment: presence of cause when outcome occurs (occurrence judgment) and outcome rate when cause is alone present (strength estimate). Judgment computed as weighted average over contingency cell frequencies with differential weights assigned to ambiguous joint-cause cells.

Feature vectors encode items in primary memory with modality-dependent and modality-independent components. Recall retrieves cues that compete via similarity-based choice rule; phonologically similar items share more features, degrading cue discriminability. Individual differences in overall performance (attention parameter) modulate effect sizes via impact on feature mismatch distance without requiring strategy shifts.
domain: Working memory, phonological similarity, word length
